text: A comprehensive review (FNAS Journal of Scientific Innovation, March 2026) surveyed AI applications across modern astrophysics — covering machine learning for exoplanet detection (transit photometry, radial velocity), galaxy morphology classification (Hubble sequence, JWST deep fields), gravitational wave detection (LIGO/Virgo glitch classification), and transient astronomy (real-time classification of supernovae, gamma-ray bursts) — documenting that CNNs now process data from Vera Rubin LSST, JWST, and SKA telescopes at petabyte scale.

A University of Warwick study (ScienceDaily, May 2026) used a powerful AI pipeline to reanalyze NASA Kepler and TESS archival data, discovering 100+ previously undetected exoplanets — including 15 rare multi-planet systems and several Earth-sized worlds in habitable zones — demonstrating that AI can extract discoveries from already-analyzed data that human and traditional statistical methods missed, effectively "doubling" the scientific return of billion-dollar space telescope investments.
